metaTitle: The Boeing Atmosphere Global Documentation Hub - Components - Text fields

header.title: Text field
header.description: The text field lets users enter text into a UI

items: Make sure text fields look interactive
The text field’s state (blank, with input, error, etc) should be visible at a glance
Keep labels and error messages brief and easy to act on
Text fields commonly appear in forms and dialogs

data.resource: Figma: Atmosphere Foundations and Components Library

sections.textBefore:
States

States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element.

Error states

Error states are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element. An error message can display instructions on how to fix it. Error messages are displayed below the text field as supporting text until fixed.

sections.text:
Measurements

This overview delves into the key dimensions of text fields

sections.imgAlt: Padding and size measurements without icons
Padding and size measurements with supporting text and character count
sections.textAfter: Padding and size measurements with supporting text and character count

sections.imgAlt: Padding and size measurements with supporting text and character count
sections.textAfter: Padding and size measurements with supporting text and character count

sections.text:
Density

Text fields component comes in two density modes: relaxed and condensed.

sections.textBefore:
Configurations

Text fields can be configured to accommodate a wide range of data types to meet diverse user needs. Here's a closer look at some common configurations:
sections.textAfter:
1. With supporting text
  2. With trailing icon
  3. With leading icon
  4. With leading icon and trailing icon
  5. With prefix
  6. With suffix
  7. Text Area

sections.text:
Usage

Use a text field when someone needs to enter text into a UI, such as filling in contact or payment information. For a better understanding of input usage in forms read our form documentation pattern.

Container

Containers improve the discoverability of text fields by creating contrast between the text field and surrounding content.

A text field container has a fill and a stroke (either around the entire container, or just the bottom edge). The color and thickness of a stroke can change to indicate when the text field is active.

The container of an outlined text field has rounded corners.

Label text

Label text informs users about what information is requested for a text field. Every text field should have a label.

Label text should be aligned with the input text, and always visible. It can be placed in the middle of a text field, or rest near the top of the container.

Label text shouldn't be truncated or take up multiple lines. Keep it short, clear, and fully visible.
sections.imgText: Label text should always be visible. When the field is selected, the label text moves from the middle of the text field to the top

images.text: Don’t truncate label text. Keep it short, clear, and fully visible
Label text shouldn’t take up multiple lines

sections.text:
Required text indicator

To indicate that a field is required, display an asterisk (*) next to the label text and mention in supporting text that the asterisks indicate required fields.

  • If some fields are required, indicate all required ones
  • If most fields are required, indicate optional fields by displaying the word “optional” in parentheses next to the label text
  • If required text has a particular color, that color should also be used for the asterisk

Tooltips

Tooltips are best when they provide additional explanations for a form field unfamiliar to some users or reasoning for what may seem like an unusual request.

  • Remember that tooltips disappear, so instructions or other directly actionable information, like field requirements, shouldn’t be in a tooltip.
  • Provide brief and helpful content inside the tooltip. Tooltips with obvious or redundant text are not beneficial to users. If you can’t think of particularly helpful content, don’t offer a tooltip.

Input text

Input text is text the user has entered into a text field. Text fields can display user input text in the following ways:

  • Single line text fields display only one line of text, the text will truncate.
  • Text areas are fixed-height fields

When the text entered by the user exceeds the maximum width of the text field, it will scroll while the user is typing. Once the user has entered the complete text, it will return to its initial position and an ellipsis will be displayed. If the user taps on the input again, it will activate, showing the end of the entered text with the text cursor active. If the width of the field is shorter than the length of the label, the label will add an ellipsis.
sections.imgText: Input text in a filled text field

sections.text:
Text area

Text area fields can work with or without a character limit. In the case where there is a limit, we will always show the character counter with what has been written and the maximum amount of text allowed.

Text area fields can work with or without a character limit. In the case where there is a limit, we will always show the character counter with what has been written and the maximum amount of text allowed.

images.text: In single-line fields, as the cursor reaches the right field edge, text longer than the input line automatically scrolls left. Single-line fields are not suitable for collecting long responses. For those, use a multi-line text field or text area instead.

Text areas are taller than text fields and wrap overflow text onto a new line. They are a fixed height and scroll vertically when the cursor reaches the bottom of the field.  The large initial size indicates that longer responses are possible and encouraged.  These should be used instead of multi-line fields on the web. Ensure the height of a text area fits within mobile screen sizes.

sections.text:
Affix: prefix and suffix text

Text fields can contain both prefix and suffix text, which is useful for indicating specific types of input. For example, a currency symbol can be used as a prefix to denote monetary values, while a unit of measurement can be used as a suffix to indicate the expected input format.

These features help users understand the expected input format and provide contextual information directly within the text field.

  • Text fields can contain prefix text such as a currency symbol.
  • Text fields can contain suffix text such as unit of measurement or email domain.

Please note that inputs intended to represent currency with the corresponding currency symbol will do so using this prefix composition. They will not include this symbol as an icon from the library.
sections.imgText: A text field with a currency symbol text prefix

sections.imgAlt: A text field with a metric scale as suffix
sections.imgText: A text field with a metric scale as suffix

sections.text:
Supporting messages

Supporting messages convey additional information about the input field, such as how it will be used. It should ideally only take up a single line, though may wrap to multiple lines if required, and be either persistently visible or visible only on focus.

Character or word counters should be used if there is a character or word limit. They display the ratio of characters used and the total character limit.

Supporting text

For text fields that validate their content (such as passwords), replace supporting text with error text when applicable. Swapping supporting text with error text helps prevent new lines of text from being introduced into a layout, thus bumping content to fit it.

Elements: help text, error and character counter

Help texts provide additional information on how to complete a field within a form. They can also be used to clarify how said information will be used. Help texts should be concise and easy to read.

When the data entered does not conform to the acceptable parameters, you will encounter an error. This could be because the data has not been entered at all or has been entered incorrectly. An error message will give you instructions on how to solve the problem.

In text areas where there is a character limit, we will indicate the number of characters used and the total character limit.

Caution!

  • If the field already has help text, the error message will replace that text until the information has been entered correctly.
  • If only one error is possible, error text describes how to avoid the error.
  • If multiple errors are possible, error text describes how to avoid the most likely error.
sections.imgAlt: Elements: help text, error and character counter
sections.imgText: Swap supporting text with error text

images.text: Don't add error text in addition to supporting text, as their appearance will shift content

Long errors can wrap to multiple lines if there isn't enough space to clearly describe the error. In this case, ensure padding between text fields is sufficient to prevent multi-lined errors from bumping layout content

sections.text:
Icons

Icons in text fields are optional. Text field icons can describe valid input methods (such as a microphone icon), provide affordances to access additional functionality (such as clearing the content of a field), and can express an error.

Leading and trailing icons change their position based on LTR or RTL contexts.

We can use the icons for two purposes:

  • Contextual provide the user with greater context about the type of information they have to enter. For example, adding the credit card icon in the field where they need to enter their card number.
  • Actionable allows users to perform an additional action within the field. For example, tapping on an eye icon to show or hide a password.

Icon usage and positioning

All actionable icons (such as clear dropdown or calendar) must be placed on the right side of the input field, in what is referred to as the trailing icon space.

On the other hand, contextual icons that support the content of the input field must be placed on the left side. These icons provide context or extra information related to the input, such as user or email.

sections.textAfter:
1. Icon signifier. Icon signifiers can describe the type of input a text field requires, and be touch targets for nested components. For example, a calendar icon may be tapped to reveal a date picker.
  2. Valid or error icon. Iconography can indicate both valid and invalid inputs, making error states clear for colorblind users.
  3. Clear icon. Clear icons let users clear an entire input field. They appear only when input text is present.
  4. Voice input icon. A microphone icon signifies that users can input characters using voice.
  5. Dropdown icon. A dropdown arrow indicates that a text field has a nested selection component.

sections.text:
Read-only fields

Read-only text fields display pre-filled text that the user cannot edit. A read-only text field is styled the same as a regular text field and is clearly labeled as read-only.

sections.textAfter:
1. Container
  2. Leading icon (optional)
  3. Label text (empty)
  4. Label text (populated)
  5. Trailing icon (optional)
  6. Caret
  7. Input text
  8. Supporting text (optional)

sections.textBefore:
Responsive layouts states

As layouts adapt to larger screens and different aspect ratios, apply flexible container dimensions to text fields. Set minimum and maximum values for margins, padding, and container dimensions as layouts scale so that typography adjusts for better reading experiences.

sections.text: As text fields expand within fluid layouts, avoid maintaining fixed margins and typography properties because this can lead to extra long text fields. Text fields should not, for example, span the full width of a large screen.
sections.imgAlt: Text fields shouldn’t span the full width of a large screen
sections.imgText: Don’t use fixed text field margins on large devices. Text fields shouldn’t span the full width of a large screen

sections.text:
Use cases

Users should be able to:

  • Navigate to and activate a text field with assistive technology
  • Input information into the text field
  • Receive and understand supporting text and error messages
  • Navigate to and select interactive icons

Interaction & style

Changes to color and thickness of stroke help provide clear visual cues for interaction.

Containers improve the discoverability of text fields by creating contrast between the text field and surrounding content.

In some contexts, outlined text fields can improve the perception of the fields with a 3:1 or greater contrast ratio between the container outline and the background.
sections.imgText: Interaction between enabled and focus states

sections.text: Keyboard navigation

Labeling elements

If the UI text is correctly linked, assistive tech (such as a screenreader) will read the UI text followed by the component’s role.

The accessibility label for a text field is typically the same as the label for the text field.
sections.imgText: A text field’s label should include its UI text

sections.text:
For text fields with interactive trailing icons, the accessibility label clarifies its function.

For example, when a password is hidden, the label for the view icon is "Show password," and when the password is visible, the label is "Hide password."

When an icon has no actionable role, like an error icon, the label is "Error."
sections.imgAlt: When a trailing icon in the field acts as a button, the label should clarify function, while the role explains the component type
sections.imgText: When a trailing icon in the field acts as a button, the label should clarify function, while the role explains the component type

sections.text:
The prefix and suffix of a text field provides symbols and abbreviations to help users enter the correct values.

The accessibility label for prefix and suffix needs to have a unique id attribute, for example, the currency name for a currency symbol prefix.
sections.imgAlt: A form containing fields with both a prefix for currency, and a suffix for email address
sections.imgText: A form containing fields with both a prefix for currency, and a suffix for email address

sections.text:
When there is an error, "alert" is applied to the role and the error message to the text label.

If a text field displays both supporting text and error text, the label should include the supporting text first, followed by the error text.
sections.imgAlt: Text field error messages should be given an “alert” role in accessibility labels
sections.imgText: Text field error messages should be given an “alert” role in accessibility labels

sections.text: The accessibility label for the character counter clarifies the number of characters that can be entered into the text field.
sections.imgAlt: The remaining character counter should be called “character count” within the label
sections.imgText: The remaining character counter should be called “character count” within the label

sections.text: The text displayed in the supporting text is also used for its accessibility label.
sections.imgAlt: Text field supporting text should have its own accessibility label
sections.imgText: Text field supporting text should have its own accessibility label

sections.text: If a text field requires input, indicate so with an asterisk at the end of the text field label. The accessibility label must include the asterisk.
sections.imgAlt: A required text field’s accessibility label should include any supporting text
sections.imgText: A required text field’s accessibility label should include any supporting text
